title: Genesis: HOOP Implementation
description:
# Genesis: HOOP Implementation

**Tied to plan:** `/home/coding/HOOP/docs/plan/plan.md` (23 sections)

## What HOOP is

A single Rust daemon that runs on a long-lived operator host and serves as the human-facing interface to a multi-project NEEDLE worker fleet. HOOP reads artifacts across projects (beads, events, conversations, files, costs, capacity) and writes exactly one kind of record: beads, via `br create`, when the operator or the human-interface agent drafts new work.

HOOP's unique position is the *join* across `projects × Stitches × conversations × files × cost × time`. Its value is making that join legible to a human operator.

## Core invariants (do not break)

1. **`br create` is HOOP's only write.** No bead close/update/release. No tmux control. No worker lifecycle. No capacity enforcement.
2. **Events are authoritative; projections derived.** Never cache to disk what can be rebuilt from events.
3. **Liveness = process, never file.** `kill -0 pid && !stopped_record`.
4. **Humans work in Stitches.** Bead IDs appear only in expert/audit views.
5. **Lazy context for the agent.** Thin index by default; tool calls for details on demand.
6. **Stitches descend into obscurity by recency; they do not close.**
7. **LLM-agnostic agent.** Adapter config matches NEEDLE's; switchable without code changes.
8. **Security inherited from environment.** No custom auth; SSH + Tailscale are the trust root.

## Phase checklist

- [x] Phase 0: Foundation (docs scaffolding, notes, plan)
- [ ] Phase 1: Single-host daemon, one workspace, read-only (v0.1)
- [ ] Phase 2: Multi-project observability + marquee features (v0.2)
- [ ] Phase 3: File browser + artifact preview + multimodal (v0.3)
- [ ] Phase 4: Bead creation interface (v0.4)
- [ ] Phase 5: Human-interface agent (v0.5)
- [ ] Phase 6: Operational polish (v0.6)
- [ ] Phase 7: Multi-operator (v1.0)

## Cross-cutting concerns

- §12 Onboarding & documentation
- §13 Security model (inherited from environment)
- §14 Testing strategy (with `testrepo/` dummy fixture)
- §15 Backups & disaster recovery (S3-compatible, B2 default)
- §16 Self-observability (Prometheus metrics, `/debug/state`)
- §17 Configuration precedence & hot-reload (config.yml + env vars)
- §18 Privacy & redaction
- §19 Multi-operator concurrency (phase 7)
- §20 Schema evolution (SemVer)
- §21 Mobile UX (responsive from day one)
- §22 Extensibility (skills / scripts / notes / prompts)

## Vocabulary (canonical — do not reintroduce removed terms)

- **Project** — logical unit, contains one or more Workspaces
- **Workspace** — single repo with its own `.beads/`
- **Stitch** — single conversation within a project (operator / dictated / worker / ad-hoc)
- **Pattern** — optional cross-project grouping of Stitches toward a goal
- **Bead** — NEEDLE's execution unit; HOOP abstracts it
- **Human-interface agent** — persistent conversational role (was "Mayor" — removed)
- **Reflection Ledger** — learned-rules store

Removed vocabulary (do not reintroduce): Mayor, polecat, swarm (as term), convoy, Gas Town lineage.

## References

- Plan: `docs/plan/plan.md`
- Onboarding: `README.md`
- LLM guide: `AGENTS.md`
- Prior-art notes: `docs/notes/{reference-feature-inventory,architecture-patterns,interop-with-needle,orchestrator-problems-and-solutions}.md`

## Closing criteria

Close this genesis bead when all seven phase epics close with success criteria met and public README published (phase 7 v1.0 target).

# Phase 0: Foundation

**Status:** COMPLETE as of 2026-04-22.

## What landed
- Repo `jedarden/HOOP` created and public

The plan is authoritative. Every downstream phase's implementation refers back to specific sections of the plan for rationale. The prior-art notes capture lessons distilled from mature multi-agent control planes, so we can skip reinventing fixes for problems they already solved.

This bead is immediately closable — everything in scope is already done. It exists as a record that foundation predates all other work.

title: X: Security model (§13)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Security model

**Plan section:** §13

## Posture
**Security inherited from the environment.** HOOP does not implement its own authentication layer. It binds to localhost (SSH-tunnel accessed) or the Tailscale interface. Whoever can reach the port is authenticated via the operator's pre-existing trust root (SSH keys, Tailscale ACLs, OS user).

## What HOOP still owns
- Path-traversal hardening on every filesystem operation
- Attachment sanitization (SVG XSS, PDF metadata, content-sniffed file types)
- Audit-log append-only + daily hash chain for tamper evidence
- Secrets scanning on attachments and transcripts (details in §18)
- MCP socket on Unix domain socket with same user:group (not TCP)
- Regex-validate every wire ID before filesystem use (`^bd-[a-z0-9]+$`, etc.)

## Explicit non-authentication patterns
- No password prompts
- No session cookies
- No JWT verification
- No IdP integration

## References
- Plan §13 Security model
- Plan §18 Privacy & redaction (related)
- Notes: orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §K

## Closing criteria
- All path-derived operations use canonical allowlist matching
- Audit log hash chain verifiable
- Secrets scanner integrated into attachment pipeline
- MCP socket permissions tested
- No custom auth code path exists

title: Path-traversal hardening: canonicalize + allowlist every wire-derived path
description:
## Task
Every filesystem operation whose path derives from a wire ID canonicalizes and matches against a pre-computed allowlist of acceptable roots (project workspace, project `.beads/`, attachment dir).

## Acceptance
- Regex-validate wire IDs before use (`^bd-[a-z0-9]+$`, `^stitch-[a-z0-9]+$`, `^[a-z]+$` for worker names)
- `realpath` resolution then prefix-match against allowlist
- Rejection returns 400 with safe error (don't echo bad path)
- Test fixtures include 10 path-traversal attack vectors; all rejected

## Plan reference
§13 Security, §K2 from orchestrator-problems-and-solutions

title: Attachment sanitization: SVG strip scripts, PDF metadata validate, content-sniff file types
description:
## Task
Every uploaded attachment goes through sanitization:
- SVG: strip `<script>`, event handlers, embedded foreign content
- PDF: validate metadata fields against known-safe schema
- All: content-sniff the type (magic bytes), not the extension
- Reject mismatch between claimed and actual type

## Acceptance
- SVG with script tag rejected with clear error
- Mismatch (claimed PNG, actual EXE) rejected
- PDF with embedded JS sanitized to no-JS version
- Tests cover each attack class

## Plan reference
§13 Security attachment sanitization

title: Audit log hash chain: each row hashes prior row content
description:
## Task
actions table carries `hash_prev` and `hash_self`. Each row's hash includes prior row's hash, providing tamper evidence for post-hoc review.

## Acceptance
- Hash function: SHA-256
- First row's prev = genesis constant
- Verification command: `hoop audit verify` walks chain, reports tampering
- Backup manifest includes final hash as integrity anchor

## Plan reference
§13 Security audit hash chain

title: MCP socket permissions: Unix domain socket, same user:group, not TCP
description:
## Task
The MCP server (phase 5) binds to a UDS at `~/.hoop/mcp.sock`. Mode 0600. Only processes running as the same OS user can connect.

## Acceptance
- Socket mode verified on startup
- Binding fails cleanly if mode cannot be set
- Zero TCP exposure for MCP
- Smoke test: another user can't connect

## Plan reference
§13 Security (MCP on UDS), §6 Phase 5 (MCP server)

title: X: Testing strategy & testrepo fixture (§14)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Testing strategy

**Plan section:** §14

## Foundation: the `testrepo/` dummy project

The HOOP repo ships a realistic dummy workspace at `testrepo/`. Every integration and E2E test operates against `testrepo/` — no live NEEDLE, no live CLI, no live LLM required.

Contents:
- Rust crate + docs + config (~500 files)
- Pre-populated `.beads/` with synthetic beads in known states
- Pre-recorded CLI session JSONL per adapter (Claude / Codex / OpenCode / Gemini / Aider) with proper tag prefixes
- Canned `events.jsonl` and `heartbeats.jsonl`
- Example attachments (image, audio, video) for multimodal tests
- `br` stub binary checked in, records calls without requiring a real install

## Test layers
1. Unit — pure functions (event parsers, tag extraction, path canonicalization, cost math, capacity arithmetic, Stitch status derivation, schema version negotiation)
2. Integration — daemon boots, tails fixtures, serves WS, test client drives + asserts
3. E2E — full `hoop init` + project reg + Stitch creation + `br create` + UI assertions
4. Property (`proptest`) — invariants: events in-order, derived status monotonic, replay-from-disk equals live
5. Load — 20 projects × 5 workers × 200 beads synthetic; UI responsiveness, memory, fan-out
6. UI (Playwright) — headless browser vs production binary; desktop + mobile viewports

## Deliberate non-test
- Real `br` version matrix (pin minimum, one version)
- Real LLM (use golden transcripts; opt-in suite for releases)
- Real Tailscale (use localhost in CI)

## References
- Plan §14 Testing strategy
- Plan §10 Milestones (phase success criteria → tests)

## Closing criteria
- `testrepo/` committed and populated with realistic fixtures
- Every phase's success criteria translate to automated tests running green in CI
- Load test harness runs in CI; budget violations block merge

title: X: Backups & disaster recovery (§15)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Backups & disaster recovery

**Plan section:** §15

## Scope
Everything HOOP owns under `~/.hoop/`:
- `config.yml` — non-secret configuration
- `projects.yaml` — project registry
- `fleet.db` — audit, Stitches, Patterns, Reflection Ledger
- `attachments/` — audio, images, video, screen captures
- `skills/`, `scripts/`, `notes/`, `prompts/` — operator extensions
- `templates/` — Stitch templates

Explicitly out of scope: bead state (`br`'s job), NEEDLE state (separate), CLI session files (each CLI's), git state (git's).

## Target
Configurable **S3-compatible endpoint** (B2 default, AWS S3, MinIO, Garage, any S3 API). Credentials via env vars; endpoint + bucket in `config.yml`. Matches existing environment's ARMOR-encrypted-B2 pattern.

## What gets backed up
- `fleet.db` daily via `VACUUM INTO` snapshot → compressed + optionally `age`-encrypted → upload
- Attachments incremental sync (only new/changed since last success)
- Config files on every change plus daily
- Each run writes a `manifest.json` with schema version + piece list

## Restore
- `hoop restore --from s3://<bucket>/<prefix>/<snapshot-id>`
- Idempotent; precondition: `hoop serve` stopped
- Moves existing `~/.hoop/` aside for rollback before restore

## DR scenarios covered
- Disk death
- `fleet.db` corruption
- Accidental deletion
- Host migration

## References
- Plan §15
- Memory: `project_armor_backup_strategy.md` (ARMOR encrypted S3 proxy for B2)

## Closing criteria
- Backup runs on schedule; credentials validated
- Restore from recent snapshot produces identical state (verified)
- Documentation covers all four DR scenarios
- age encryption works with key in env var

title: X: Self-observability — metrics & /debug (§16)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Self-observability

**Plan section:** §16

## Why
A daemon that reads everyone's files, shells to `br`, spawns Whisper, and drives an LLM has many internal states. Without metrics, operators can't diagnose HOOP itself.

## Surfaces
- `/metrics` — Prometheus-format
- `/debug/state` — JSON endpoint with fleet roster, open Stitches, agent sessions, every pid, every WS client, config hash, backup timestamps
- Local-only binding by default

## Metric categories (from plan §16)
16.1 Operational — uptime, memory, FDs, panics, errors
16.2 Event ingestion — tailer lag per project/adapter, heartbeat freshness, unknown-event counter
16.3 WebSocket/HTTP — client count, broadcast lag, HTTP route stats
16.4 Bead/Stitch — `br` success/latency, Stitch/bead creation rate, audit append rate, orphan count
16.5 Agent/AI — turn duration, tool call success, token usage, Whisper duration, Reflection proposal/approval rates
16.6 Storage — db size, WAL, attachments, migration duration, backup timestamps
16.7 Business — cost per Stitch, anomaly alerts, dedup hits, capacity warnings

## References
- Plan §16
- Notes: orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §M (observability gaps)

## Closing criteria
- `/metrics` serves all catalogued metrics
- `/debug/state` shows every piece of runtime state needed for triage
- Prometheus scrape smoke-tested
- Dashboards (Grafana-compatible JSON) included in repo for import

title: X: Configuration & hot-reload (§17)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Configuration & hot-reload

**Plan section:** §17

## Precedence (deterministic)
```
CLI flags > env vars > config.yml > compiled defaults
```

## Layout
- `~/.hoop/config.yml` — non-secret configuration (the single source of truth for almost everything)
- Env vars — secrets only (API keys, S3 credentials, age encryption keys); never in audit log or default-verbosity logs
- CLI flags — bootstrap only (`hoop serve`'s config path, socket path, log level)

## Config.yml sections
- `agent:` — adapter/model/rate limits/cost cap
- `projects_file:` — projects.yaml path
- `backup:` — endpoint/bucket/prefix/schedule/retention
- `ui:` — theme, archive-after-days, default sort
- `voice:` — Whisper model, hotkey, max record seconds
- `agent_extensions:` — skills/scripts/notes/prompts dirs (§22)
- `metrics:` — `/metrics` enable + port
- `audit:` — retention, hash-chain on/off
- `reflection:` — detection thresholds, proposal auto-archive

## Hot-reload
**Everything reloads without restart**, including projects.yaml, agent adapter config, backup schedule, log verbosity, UI preferences.

Exceptions (restart required):
- Socket paths, listen addresses
- `fleet.db` location

## Validation
Any edit is schema-validated BEFORE apply. Invalid edits rejected loudly (UI banner + metric + log); old config stays running. **A bad config never takes down the daemon.**

## References
- Plan §17

## Closing criteria
- All config sections hot-reload without restart
- Schema validation catches all type/bound errors
- Precedence rules implemented and documented
- Bad-config test: edit-to-invalid leaves daemon running correctly

title: X: Privacy & redaction (§18)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Privacy & redaction

**Plan section:** §18

## Attack surface
- Dictation (audio + Whisper transcript)
- Screen captures (frames + audio + transcript)
- Attachments (images, videos, PDFs)
- Agent conversations (operator speech + file contents)
- CLI session tailers (reading JSONL written by CLIs)

## Defenses
- **Secrets scanner** on every attachment upload: API keys (Stripe/OpenAI/Anthropic/AWS/GitHub patterns), env-var-looking leaks, high-entropy strings with context-aware exclusions, email PII
- **Warning not blocking** — operator sees what was flagged and chooses redact-in-place / redact-and-rewind / proceed. Nothing silent.
- **Transcript redaction with word-timestamps** — redact word in transcript + mute matching seconds in audio, atomically
- **Read-side session tailer** — HOOP's projections go through redaction filter; raw CLI JSONL files never mutated
- **Log hygiene** — WARN default; INFO/DEBUG also redacted

## Operator control
- `config.yml` policy: pattern sets, entropy threshold, action (warn / redact / reject)
- Per-project override (strict mode for customer-data projects)
- Audit trail of every redaction

## What HOOP cannot prevent
CLI agents write their own session JSONL; secrets there are in the CLI's native cache, not HOOP's responsibility to clean.

## References
- Plan §18
- Plan §13 (security model — related)

## Closing criteria
- Secrets scanner catches common token formats in test fixtures
- Word-level audio muting verified end-to-end
- Log lines redacted at default verbosity
- Per-project policy override tested
- Audit trail for redactions complete

title: Secrets scanner for attachments and transcripts
description:
## Task
Pre-storage scan of every attachment and transcript. Detects:
- Common secret patterns (Stripe, OpenAI, Anthropic, AWS, GitHub, JWT)
- Env-var-style leaks (`OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...`)
- High-entropy strings >N bits, with context-aware exclusions (git SHAs fine, other high-entropy suspicious)
- Email addresses per operator-configured PII patterns

## Behavior
**Flag, not block**. Operator sees warning banner listing findings, chooses: redact-in-place, redact-and-rewind, proceed.

## Acceptance
- Synthetic secrets in fixtures caught correctly
- False positive rate <5% on `testrepo/` content
- Redaction is atomic (transcript word + audio timestamp mute)
- Config: policy set (pattern library, action defaults) in `config.yml`

## Plan reference
§18 Privacy & redaction

title: Transcript word-level audio muting on redaction
description:
## Task
When the operator redacts a word from a Whisper transcript, mute the matching audio timestamps. Both operations atomic.

## Acceptance
- Word-level timestamps from Whisper preserved
- Audio mute implemented via ffmpeg filter or direct byte manipulation
- Transcript shows `[REDACTED]`; audio plays silence for that span
- Reversible only from audit log (no accidental undo)

## Plan reference
§18.2 Transcript redaction

title: X: Multi-operator concurrency (§19) — primarily phase 7
description:
# Cross-cutting: Multi-operator concurrency

**Plan section:** §19

## Scope
The specifics of two+ operators using the same HOOP concurrently. Primarily lands in phase 7 but shapes earlier data-model decisions.

## Specifics
1. **Draft concurrency** — drafts are server-persisted from the moment the form opens, not just on save. Two concurrent drafts both land.
2. **Reflection Ledger concurrency** — proposals deduplicated on create (content-hash). Approvals are single-operator actions.
3. **Agent session ownership** — one session per operator. No shared agents.
4. **Presence** — optional per-project and per-Stitch presence indicators, privacy-toggleable.
5. **Conflict resolution** — not a lock; two Stitches on the same workspace both land. Already-Started Detection (marquee #8) warns operators if they're duplicating.

## References
- Plan §19
- Plan §6 Phase 7
- Plan §13 (identity via Tailscale whois)

## Closing criteria
- Two concurrent drafts don't clobber each other
- Presence indicators optional and privacy-respecting
- Proposal dedup tested with same-content-hash collisions
- Per-operator agent sessions isolated correctly

title: X: Schema evolution (SemVer) (§20)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Schema evolution (SemVer)

**Plan section:** §20

## Policy
All durable records carry `schema_version: "X.Y.Z"`. Semantic Versioning applies:

- **Major** — breaking, no backwards compat. One-way migration. Upgrade-gate: `hoop migrate --from-1 --confirm` required.
- **Minor** — additive, backwards-compat. Old records read correctly; new records may have fields old readers ignore.
- **Patch** — bug fix, no shape change.

## Deprecation window
- Minor deprecations readable at least one full minor version after introduction.
- Major deprecations are one-way; operator consciously accepts at upgrade gate.

## Interaction with `br`
HOOP pins a minimum compatible `br` major version. A `br` major bump triggers HOOP's compatibility audit.

## Backup manifest includes schema version
`hoop restore` refuses newer-than-current snapshots.

## References
- Plan §20
- Plan §2.1 (`br` dependency, version pinning)

## Closing criteria
- Every record type emits `schema_version`
- Minor deprecation path documented (one full version window)
- Major upgrade gate implemented with explicit flag
- Restore refuses newer snapshots
- Schema source of truth in `hoop-schema/` with Rust + TS codegen

title: Schema crate: JSON Schema → Rust via typify + TS via json-schema-to-typescript
description:
## Task
`hoop-schema/` crate with every schema as draft-07 JSON. Build pipeline generates:
- Rust types via `typify` in build.rs
- TS types via `json-schema-to-typescript` in pre-build

## Acceptance
- Round-trip test: serialize Rust → deserialize Rust; serialize Rust → parse TS; equal
- Schema version on every record
- Added schemas land as minor bumps; breaking changes require major bump (§20)

## Plan reference
§20 Schema evolution, §7 Tech decisions

title: Schema migration framework + hoop migrate command
description:
## Task
Migration framework for fleet.db. Each migration: up (forward), down (rollback where possible), schema version.

`hoop migrate` runs pending migrations; major upgrades require `--major-upgrade --confirm`.

## Acceptance
- Migrations idempotent
- Rollback where possible (phase 6 minor bumps); one-way for major bumps
- `hoop migrate status` shows pending migrations
- Backup manifest refuses newer-than-current snapshots (§20)

## Plan reference
§20 Schema migration

title: X: Mobile UX (§21)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Mobile UX

**Plan section:** §21

## Why
The environment has a Pixel 6 on Tailscale (ADB integrated). Phone is a first-class input surface, not an afterthought.

## Breakpoints
- Phone portrait ≥ 375px (Pixel 6 native: 412px)
- Phone landscape ~700px
- Tablet ≥ 768px
- Desktop ≥ 1280px (primary target)

## Mobile-optimized flows
- Morning Brief viewing (card-per-headline, swipe, one-tap approve/redirect)
- Dictation (large mic, push-to-talk, haptic feedback, transcript preview)
- Stitch list (compact cards, project/title/activity/indicator)
- Agent chat (optimized composer, native attachment picker)
- File browser (read-only on phone; syntax highlighted, side-scrolling)
- Approval dialogs (oversized, high contrast)

## Not-for-phone (gracefully degrade)
- Stitch Net-Diff viewer (directs to desktop)
- Multi-project time-range dashboards (compact summary only)
- Reflection Ledger curation (list on phone, edit on desktop)

## Testing
Mobile UX part of v0.1 success criteria. Playwright at phone viewport sizes; visual regressions block CI.

## References
- Plan §21
- Plan §6 Phase 3 (dictation + multimodal)

## Closing criteria
- All target breakpoints tested
- Mobile-optimized flows verified on Pixel 6 via ADB
- Desktop-only surfaces have graceful degradation copy
- Playwright mobile-viewport suite in CI

title: Mobile responsive layout pass across every phase's UI components
description:
## Task
Phase-spanning task: ensure every UI component renders correctly at phone portrait (375px+), phone landscape (~700px), tablet (768px+), desktop (1280px+). Phone-specific flows (Morning Brief cards, dictation button) have optimized layouts.

## Acceptance
- Playwright suite runs at each breakpoint
- Not-for-phone surfaces (Stitch Net-Diff, multi-project dashboards) show graceful "view on desktop" message
- Touch-optimized controls (44px min tap target)
- Dictation hotkey prominent on phone

## Plan reference
§21 Mobile UX

title: X: Extensibility — skills, scripts, notes, prompts (§22)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Extensibility (skills / scripts / notes / prompts)

**Plan section:** §22

## Philosophy
HOOP is extensible without code changes. Four directory-based plugin types modeled on OpenClaw/Hermes and Claude Code's skills pattern.

## Plugin types

### Skills — `~/.hoop/skills/<name>/`
- Custom agent tools (directory with `manifest.yml` + executable `run` + optional README)
- Auto-discovered on startup and directory watch
- Agent's tool belt augmented dynamically
- Examples: `lookup-deploy-status`, `fetch-prod-logs-summary`

### Scripts — `~/.hoop/scripts/<name>`
- Executable files, any language
- Triggered: operator-invoked (UI button or `hoop script run`), event-triggered (manifest), scheduled (cron)
- Full operator privileges (HOOP user)
- **This is the escape hatch for external integrations** (replaces non-goal #13's forbidden built-in integrations)

### Notes — `~/.hoop/notes/<name>.md` (also per-project `<project>/.hoop/notes/`)
- Plain markdown; agent reads via `read_note(name)`
- Project glossaries, team conventions, reference material

### Prompts — `~/.hoop/prompts/<name>.md`
- Reusable prompt bodies, referenced by `@prompt:<name>`
- Parameter substitution: `{{project}}`, `{{file}}`, `{{stitch}}`

## Discovery & hot-reload
All four file-watched. New skill → agent's next turn has the new tool. Same for scripts/notes/prompts.

## Security
Skills and scripts run with HOOP user's privileges. No sandboxing. Operator owns their extensions. Audit log records every skill invocation with arguments.

## Sharing
Flat files → plain git clone into the dir. Tarballs. Community registry if anyone builds one (not HOOP's problem).

## References
- Plan §22

## Closing criteria
- All four plugin dirs hot-reloadable
- Skill manifest schema documented with examples
- Script triggers (manual/event/cron) all functional
- Audit log captures every skill invocation
- Quickstart example: one working skill, one script, one note, one prompt in the docs

title: Skills plugin infrastructure: ~/.hoop/skills/<name>/ with manifest + run binary
description:
## Task
Extensibility — skill directories with `manifest.yml` + executable `run`. Auto-discovered on startup and file-watched.

## Acceptance
- Manifest schema documented with example
- Skill registration auto-augments agent's tool-belt
- Hot-reload on directory change
- Audit logs every invocation with args + result
- Example skill shipped: `lookup-git-log` or similar

## Plan reference
§22 Extensibility — Skills

title: Scripts plugin: event-triggered + scheduled + operator-invoked execution
description:
## Task
Single executable files in `~/.hoop/scripts/`. Triggered via: operator (`hoop script run <name>`), events (manifest-defined), cron (manifest-defined).

## Acceptance
- Three trigger types all functional
- Full operator privilege (HOOP user)
- Audit log captures invocation (args, not stdout/stderr)
- Example scripts shipped: `notify-pushover`, `github-pr-status` (external integration escape hatch)

## Plan reference
§22 Extensibility — Scripts (replaces non-goal #13 for integrations)

title: Notes + Prompts plugins: agent-readable markdown + reusable prompt library
description:
## Task
`~/.hoop/notes/<name>.md` → agent reads via `read_note(name)` tool.
`~/.hoop/prompts/<name>.md` → operator and agent reference via `@prompt:<name>`, with parameter substitution (`{{project}}`, `{{file}}`, `{{stitch}}`).

## Acceptance
- Both hot-reloadable
- Per-project versions at `<project>/.hoop/notes/` and `<project>/.hoop/prompts/`
- Agent's tool-belt gains `read_note` and `list_prompts`
- Parameter substitution tested

## Plan reference
§22 Extensibility — Notes + Prompts

title: Phase 1: Single-host daemon, one workspace, read-only (v0.1)
description:
# Phase 1: Single-host daemon, one workspace, read-only (v0.1)

**Version target:** v0.1 | **Timeline:** +4 weeks from phase start

## Goal
HOOP runs on an EX44-class host as a pure observer of one workspace. Serves a web UI showing bead state, worker liveness, conversations, events. Zero write paths.

## Why this phase exists
Prove the full end-to-end loop works (event tailing → join → UI) before adding breadth. A broken single-project loop doesn't get better by adding more projects.

## What "done" looks like
- Rust binary builds, runs as systemd user service, serves UI on Tailscale hostname
- Observes an existing NEEDLE fleet without affecting it
- Restart → reconstructs state entirely from disk in <5s for 500 beads
- Zero silent drops in any parser
- Every bead visible in the UI; worker transcripts show bead IDs via tag-join

## Success criteria (from plan §6 Phase 1)
- HOOP runs alongside a NEEDLE fleet without affecting it
- Killing HOOP does nothing to the fleet
- Every bead visible with worker transcripts joined
- Zero silent drops — unknown events appear in diagnostic panel

## Non-goals
- Multi-project (phase 2)
- Any write path (phase 4)
- Cost/capacity panels (phase 2)
- Dependency graph (phase 2)

## Scope invariants (for this phase)
- Enforce zero-write in code: no code path calls `br` with any verb other than read verbs
- Bead-creation UI explicitly disabled
- Audit log schema exists but has no writes

## References
- Plan §6 Phase 1 — deliverables and success criteria
- Plan §4 — component architecture
- Plan §5 — data flows
- Notes: all four files in `docs/notes/` apply

## Closing criteria
All child beads closed + success criteria verified in CI against `testrepo/` fixture.

title: Scaffold Rust crate workspace + embedded-static-assets axum setup
description:
## Task
Initialize the Rust workspace for HOOP. Single binary crate. `axum` for HTTP/WS, embedded static assets for the web client.

## Why
Everything downstream depends on this. Target: `cargo build` produces a binary that starts, opens a listen socket, and serves a health endpoint.

## Acceptance
- `cargo new --bin hoop` equivalent structure in place
- `axum` + `tokio` + `tracing` dependencies pinned
- Embedded static asset module stubbed (phase 1 UI will ship here)
- `hoop serve` subcommand starts and listens
- `/healthz` returns 200

## Notes
- Crate layout: workspace with `hoop-cli`, `hoop-daemon`, `hoop-schema`, `hoop-ui` (embedded)
- Follow Rust edition 2021+
- Cargo.lock committed

## Plan reference
§7 Tech decisions, §4.1 Daemon subcommands

title: Session tailer: Gemini adapter (~/.gemini/tmp/)
description:
## Task
Gemini CLI session tailer. Handle sandbox-root variations (GEMINI_CLI_HOME).

## Acceptance
- Discovers sessions under configured home
- Parses native usage fields
- Registry-based path resolution per §A1

## Plan reference
§4.3, notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §A1

title: Session tailer: Aider adapter
description:
## Task
Aider tailer. Simpler shape (no session continuity beyond git); parser maps `--message` invocations and outputs.

## Acceptance
- Identifies Aider sessions
- Extracts per-message stats
- Prompt-tag extraction still works

## Plan reference
§4.3

title: Tag-join resolver: extract [needle:<worker>:<bead>:<strand>] prefix
description:
## Task
Every bead dispatch prefixes the first user message with `[needle:<worker>:<bead-id>:<strand>]`. The resolver extracts this on parse and establishes the session → bead mapping.

## Why
Core mechanism for joining CLI session transcripts to beads. Without it, HOOP can't show "which conversation produced this bead."

## Acceptance
- Regex validated and tested against fixtures (all four adapters)
- Missing tag → session classified as `operator` or `ad-hoc`, not auto-joined
- Malformed tag logged at warn, treated as missing
- Binding emitted as `session_bound` event (dual-identity invariant §B1)

## Plan reference
§5.1 Data flows (tag-join arrow), notes/interop-with-needle.md Hook 1, notes/reference-feature-inventory.md §1

title: Web UI scaffolding: React + Vite + TypeScript + Jotai + embedded build
description:
## Task
Stand up the client app. Vite dev server for local dev; production build produces static bundle embedded in the Rust binary.

## Acceptance
- `pnpm install` + `pnpm build` produces static assets
- Rust side embeds via `rust-embed` or similar; served from `axum` at `/`
- Dev mode: Vite proxies WS + REST to daemon port
- Hot-reload during development
- Jotai store initialized with empty atoms for `conversations`, `projects`, `stitches`

## Plan reference
§4.5 Web client, §7 Tech decisions

title: Web UI: fleet map panel (workers + current activity for single project)
description:
## Task
Render the fleet map for the single registered project. One card per worker showing: name, adapter, model, current bead title (if any), last heartbeat age, state indicator.

## Acceptance
- Updates live via WS events
- Worker state colors: executing (green), idle (gray), hung (yellow), dead (red)
- Click-through opens worker's transcript view
- Renders 10+ workers without layout break
- Zero bead IDs by default (§3.11 — humans work in Stitches)

## Plan reference
§4.5, §6 Phase 1 deliverable 5

title: Web UI: bead list panel (tabular, sortable, Expert-mode toggle shows IDs)
description:
## Task
Tabular view of all beads in the registered project. By default shows title, state, worker (if claimed), age. Expert-mode toggle reveals bead IDs and technical fields.

## Why
Phase 1 precedes the Stitch abstraction (phase 2). This panel is the literal bead view for operators who want it; once Stitches land, the primary UI pivots to Stitches and this panel becomes Expert-only.

## Acceptance
- Tabular rendering with 500+ beads performant
- Column sort stable
- Expert-mode toggle hidden behind settings
- Default view shows human-readable titles, not IDs

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 1 deliverable 5 (bead list — tabular, no graph yet)

title: Web UI: worker timeline view (per-worker Gantt-style activity)
description:
## Task
Per-worker timeline rendering. Horizontal strip per worker showing periods of bead execution + idle time + heartbeat markers.

## Acceptance
- Renders last 24h by default; configurable window
- Hover reveals bead title + duration + outcome
- Click opens transcript
- Live updates as events arrive

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 1 deliverable 5

title: Web UI: conversation viewer with fleet/ad-hoc classification
description:
## Task
Side pane that renders CLI session transcripts. Top of each view shows: project, bead id (expert view), worker or operator actor, adapter+model.

Classification: first user message has `[needle:...]` prefix → `fleet` badge. No prefix → `operator` or `ad-hoc` badge.

## Acceptance
- Streaming support for live sessions (in-flight isolation — streaming content in a separate atom)
- Historic session replay works (play back from JSONL)
- Actor and adapter badges correct for each classification
- Agent interrupt / dry-run affordances (future phases) hook into this component

## Plan reference
§5.3 Ad-hoc vs fleet conversations, §4.5 Web client, §6 Phase 1 deliverable 5

title: Web UI: audit overlay (read-only fleet.db actions log view)
description:
## Task
Modal / panel that renders HOOP's own actions audit log. Read-only.

Phase 1 has no writes, so the audit log will be empty — this panel exists to verify the schema and UI pipeline before phase 4's first writes land.

## Acceptance
- Table with ts, actor, action, target, args, result
- Filterable by project, actor, action kind
- Hash-chain verification indicator (§13 — tamper evidence)

## Plan reference
§4.5, §13 Security (audit hash chain)

title: Web UI: search palette (cmd-K; substring across conversations; 50-result cap)
description:
## Task
Global search palette invoked with cmd-K. Searches conversation content, bead titles, project names. 50-result cap, 60-character snippets around matches.

## Acceptance
- 150ms debounce
- Case-insensitive substring for v0.1 (embedding search phase 2)
- Keyboard navigation (arrow keys + Enter)
- Escape closes

## Plan reference
§4.5 Web client, notes/reference-feature-inventory.md §8

title: Implement local Unix-socket control plane at ~/.hoop/control.sock
description:
## Task
The `hoop serve` daemon exposes a local Unix-domain socket at `~/.hoop/control.sock` for other `hoop` subcommands to communicate with it. All CLI clients talk to the daemon through this socket, never directly to state.

## Why
Architectural principle: one long-lived daemon, everything else is a client. Control socket decouples CLI tooling from daemon internals.

## Acceptance
- Socket created on daemon start with user:group permissions
- Simple request/response protocol (JSON lines)
- `hoop status` client works against running daemon
- Socket cleaned up on graceful shutdown
- Permission: only same OS user can connect

## Plan reference
§4.1 Daemon subcommands (note: "other subcommands are clients over Unix socket"), §13 Security (UDS with same user:group)

title: Schema crate: JSON Schema draft-07 source of truth + Rust/TS codegen
description:
## Task
Create the `hoop-schema/` crate. JSON Schema files are source of truth; Rust types codegen via `typify`; TS types codegen via `json-schema-to-typescript`. Every record includes `schema_version: "1.0.0"`.

## Acceptance
- At least these schemas: WS messages, UI state, projects.yaml, config.yml, audit row, capacity-account, fleet.db tables (Stitch, Pattern, Reflection rows for forward compat)
- Codegen runs in cargo build.rs (Rust) and pre-build (TS)
- Round-trip test: serialize → deserialize → equal

## Plan reference
§7 Tech decisions, §20 Schema evolution, notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §L (schema migration lessons)

title: fleet.db SQLite initializer + audit table schema
description:
## Task
Initialize `~/.hoop/fleet.db` with the actions audit table. Phase 1 has no writes, but the schema lands now.

## Schema for v0.1
```
actions(id, ts, actor, kind, target, project, args_json, result, hash_prev, hash_self)
```

## Acceptance
- DB created on first daemon start
- WAL mode enabled for concurrent reads
- Hash chain initialized with genesis row
- Schema version tagged in a metadata table
- Migration framework stubbed (even if no migrations in v0.1)

## Plan reference
§4.4 Cross-project state, §13 Security (hash chain), §15 Backups (fleet.db is the primary backup target)

title: NEEDLE hook #1: prompt prefix tag (one-line PR to NEEDLE)
description:
## Task
Submit a one-line PR (or equivalent coordination) to NEEDLE: prepend dispatched prompts with `[needle:<worker>:<bead-id>:<strand>]`. That tag is how HOOP joins CLI session transcripts to beads.

## Acceptance
- PR opened against jedarden/NEEDLE
- Tag format matches HOOP's resolver regex
- NEEDLE maintainer signs off
- HOOP's integration tests pass against the hook's PR branch

## Plan reference
Notes/interop-with-needle.md (Hook 1), §5.1 Data flows

title: NEEDLE hook #2: events.jsonl append helper + heartbeat thread
description:
## Task
Also coordinated with NEEDLE: ensure each worker emits events to `.beads/events.jsonl` and heartbeats to `.beads/heartbeats.jsonl`. Standardize the event shape.

## Acceptance
- Event append helper in NEEDLE worker code (or confirmed already present)
- Heartbeat thread per worker emits every ~10s
- Event shapes match HOOP's parser expectations (documented schema)
- Integration test in HOOP's `testrepo/` validates

## Plan reference
Notes/interop-with-needle.md (Hooks 2 & 3)

title: Worker spawn-ack mechanism (each worker writes ~/.hoop/workers/<name>.ack)
description:
## Task
Each worker, on startup, writes an ack file at `~/.hoop/workers/<name>.ack` to prove successful spawn. HOOP verifies within a grace window (~10s); missing ack = spawn failed.

## Why
Prior-art lesson: tmux send-keys can silently truncate commands; `spawned successfully` can be a lie. Verify by confirmation, not by tmux return code. (§M5)

## Note
In HOOP's final scope, HOOP doesn't spawn workers. This task ensures that WHEN NEEDLE spawns a worker, HOOP can detect spawn success vs silent failure via the ack file.

## Acceptance
- Ack file location and format documented
- HOOP watches for acks and surfaces missing ones as alerts
- NEEDLE-side support (hook) for writing the ack on worker boot

## Plan reference
Notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §M5 Silent tmux-orphan channels

title: Graceful shutdown: SIGTERM → grace → SIGKILL; socket cleanup; state flush
description:
## Task
Daemon handles SIGTERM with grace period, flushes in-flight state to disk, closes socket, exits. Hard SIGKILL backstop after grace.

## Acceptance
- SIGTERM triggers shutdown sequence
- Active WS clients notified (close frame)
- In-flight event-tailer state flushed
- fleet.db checkpointed (SQLite WAL)
- Socket cleaned up
- Exit in <5s under normal load

## Plan reference
Notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §C2 (zombie processes), §3.9 (HOOP dies nothing else notices)

title: Zero-write invariant guard (code path analysis + runtime assertion)
description:
## Task
Enforce that phase 1 code has no `br create` / `br close` / `br update` / `br release` call path. Compile-time enforcement via feature flag; runtime assertion as belt-and-suspenders.

## Why
Phase 1 is strictly read-only. A stray write path that ships breaks the "HOOP is a convenience, not a dependency" invariant.

## Acceptance
- `cargo check --features=zero-write-v01` enforces no write-verb calls reachable
- Runtime check panics early if a write attempt happens (should never trigger)
- CI test fails on write-verb call in phase-1 code paths

## Plan reference
§3.8 Principle (br create is HOOP's only write), §6 Phase 1 deliverable 7 (zero-write invariant)

title: br version-pin check at startup (refuse to start on incompatible br)
description:
## Task
On daemon start, run `br --version` and compare against the pinned minimum version from `hoop-schema/br-compat.toml`. On mismatch, refuse to start with a clear diagnostic.

## Acceptance
- Pinned minimum version in a version file
- `br --version` parsed and compared
- Mismatch diagnostic includes exact expected version
- `--allow-br-mismatch` flag for dev override (documented)

## Plan reference
§2.1 br dependency, §6 Phase 1 deliverable 8
close_reason: Implemented br version-pin check at daemon startup. br --version parsed and compared against pinned minimum from br-compat.toml (0.4.0). Daemon refuses to start on mismatch. --allow-br-mismatch flag available for dev override.

title: Implement hoop audit — dependency + environment check
description:
## Task
CLI subcommand + daemon-startup check that validates HOOP's runtime prerequisites and reports any failure with the exact command to fix it.

## Checks
- `br` in PATH (`br --version` ≥ pinned minimum)
- `tmux` in PATH
- `.beads/` accessible for each registered project
- CLI session directories readable (`~/.claude/projects/`, `~/.codex/sessions/`, etc.)
- Tailscale interface available (optional — warn only)
- Disk space sufficient (≥ 1GB in `~/.hoop/`)
- systemd user-scope enabled (optional — warn only)

## Why
From prior-art analysis: 90% of "doesn't work" reports are missing dependencies. Surface at startup with the fix command inline.

## Acceptance
- `hoop audit` runs all checks and reports
- Daemon refuses to start (or starts with degraded features flagged) on critical failures
- Each failure includes the fix command verbatim
- Output machine-readable via `--json`

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 1 deliverables #10, §12 Onboarding (dependency check is first step of `hoop init`)

title: hoop projects CLI — add/list/remove (single workspace in phase 1)
description:
## Task
CLI subcommands for managing the project registry. Phase 1 supports single-project; phase 2 expands to multi-project scan and multi-workspace.

## Commands
- `hoop projects add <path>` — register a workspace by path
- `hoop projects list` — list registered projects
- `hoop projects remove <name>` — remove a project
- `hoop projects show <name>` — details of one project

## Why
The operator needs to tell HOOP what to observe. CLI-based for phase 1; UI-based in phase 2.

## Acceptance
- Registry file at `~/.hoop/projects.yaml`
- Add path validation: path exists, contains `.beads/`
- Remove is safe (leaves workspace data intact)
- List output is stable and machine-parseable via `--json`

## Plan reference
§4.1 Subcommands, §4.2 Project registry

title: Event tailer: watch .beads/events.jsonl per project (notify crate)
description:
## Task
Implement the event tailer. Watches `.beads/events.jsonl` for the registered workspace with the `notify` crate. Emits parsed events onto an internal broadcast channel.

## Why
The event log is the authoritative source for what NEEDLE workers are doing. HOOP's projections are derived from it.

## Acceptance
- `notify` watcher survives log rotation (handle file-moved events)
- Parser uses line-buffered NDJSON with partial-line carry-over (critical — see orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §F1)
- Malformed lines logged with `warn`, never silent-dropped (§M1)
- Unknown event types emit a progress event + increment `hoop_unknown_event_total` metric
- Replay-from-start on daemon restart reconstructs full state

## Plan reference
§4.3 Per-project runtime, §5.1 Data flows, §5 Principles (events authoritative)

title: Heartbeat monitor: watch .beads/heartbeats.jsonl; compute worker liveness
description:
## Task
Parse heartbeat lines from `.beads/heartbeats.jsonl`. Maintain per-worker `last_heartbeat_at` projection. Combine with `kill -0 pid` from event log's `started` rows to compute liveness.

## Why
Liveness = process + heartbeat, never file. Rule from prior-art analysis (§C1 content-aware heartbeat, §A4 ghost liveness).

## Acceptance
- Heartbeats parse without silent-drop
- Worker liveness computed as `pid_alive AND heartbeat_fresh(≤ 2×heartbeat_interval)`
- Stale workers (heartbeat old + PID alive) flagged as "hung"
- Dead workers (PID gone) flagged as "dead"
- Liveness derivation is pure — no file writes

## Plan reference
§3.2 Principle (liveness = process, never file), §4.3 Per-project runtime, notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §A4, §C1

title: Session tailer: Claude Code adapter (~/.claude/projects/)
description:
## Task
Implement the Claude Code session tailer. Discovers `.jsonl` session files under `~/.claude/projects/<hash>/`, parses them into canonical `ParsedSession` records.

## Discovery pattern
- Two-phase: stat everything + sort by mtime, then parse in parallel (concurrency ~16, batch size 100)
- 5-second background poll detects external edits
- Bootstrap interceptor aliases newly-found files back to existing session IDs to prevent duplicates

## Acceptance
- Discovers all Claude Code sessions under the target dir
- Parses usage fields (message.usage: input/output/cache-read/cache-write)
- Progressive streaming to broadcast channel (batched `conversations_updated`)
- Line-buffered parser handles split chunks (§F1)
- Filter-by-cwd to scope sessions to the registered project

## Plan reference
§4.3 Per-project runtime, §5.1 Data flows, notes/reference-feature-inventory.md §1, notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §A1-A4, §B3

title: Session tailer: Codex adapter (~/.codex/sessions/)
description:
## Task
Codex session discovery and parsing. Different directory shape (`~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/`), different event schema (`token_count`, `thread.started`).

## Acceptance
- Discover sessions in date-bucketed dirs
- Parse `token_count` events for usage accounting
- Extract `thread_id` from `thread.started` for session binding (see §B1)
- Cwd-scoping works as for Claude adapter

## Plan reference
§4.3, notes/reference-feature-inventory.md §1

title: Session tailer: OpenCode adapter (~/.local/share/opencode/)
description:
## Task
OpenCode session discovery. Separate message / part / session storage directories.

## Acceptance
- Walks `storage/{session,message,part}` correctly
- Parses per-message `tokens.{input,output,cache.{read,write}}` + `cost`
- Session binding via first-prompt capture

## Plan reference
§4.3

title: Phase 2: Multi-project + marquee observability features (v0.2)
description:
# Phase 2: Multi-project observability + cost/capacity + visual debug (v0.2)

**Version target:** v0.2 | **Timeline:** +12 weeks

## Goal
One HOOP serves every project on the host. Cross-project dashboards, cost and capacity visibility (read-only — no enforcement), visual debug of what workers did per bead. Four marquee features land alongside: Stitch + Pattern layer, Stitch-Provenance Code Archaeology, Stitch Net-Diff Viewer, Cost-Anomaly with Fix Lineage.

## Why this phase exists
Multi-project correctness is the primary design pressure of HOOP. Session discovery has to be scoped per project or queries return soup. Cost has to bucket per project. The Stitch abstraction must exist before any write path is added so bead-creation can target Stitches, not raw beads.

## What's in scope
- Project registry (multi-workspace support: project may span several repos)
- Per-project runtime isolation (failure in one doesn't cascade)
- Fleet-of-fleets dashboard + project detail views
- Cost panel (observation only; no enforcement, no routing)
- Capacity visibility (per-account 5h/7d utilization; no throttling)
- Visual debug panel (per-bead prompts/tools/state)
- Collision detector, stuck detector
- **Marquee #1:** Stitch abstraction layer (foundational)
- **Marquee #1b:** Pattern layer (optional cross-project grouping)
- **Marquee #2:** Stitch-Provenance Code Archaeology (git blame + Stitch attribution)
- **Marquee #3:** Stitch Net-Diff Viewer (aggregate diff across a Stitch cluster)
- **Marquee #4:** Cost-Anomaly with Fix Lineage

## Non-goals
- Still read-only on bead state; no `br create` from HOOP yet (phase 4)
- No steering of workers, ever
- Capacity displayed, never enforced

## Critical design anchors
- Stitches are conversations (not work items)
- Stitches descend into obscurity by recency (no closed state)
- Patterns cross projects; Stitches don't
- Multi-workspace projects are first-class

## References
- Plan §6 Phase 2
- Plan §1.6 (Stitch + Pattern hierarchy)
- Plan §4.2 (project registry, multi-workspace)
- Plan §4.7 (Stitch / Pattern / Reflection services schema)
- Notes: architecture-patterns.md, orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §A, §B, §C, §M

## Closing criteria
- Every phase-2 child bead closed
- `hoop projects scan ~/` registers every `.beads/` workspace in one command
- Cost figures match `br` within ±2%, capacity meters within ±5% of `/status`
- Net-Diff correctly assembles a 5-bead / 11-commit cluster
- Cost anomaly flags a 3σ test case
- UI dashboards contain zero bead IDs by default

title: projects.yaml multi-workspace schema + backward-compatible shorthand
description:
## Task
Extend projects.yaml to support multiple workspaces per project. Preserve single-workspace shorthand for migration.

## Schema
```yaml
projects:
  - name: <required, unique>
    label: <optional>
    color: <optional hex>
    workspaces:
      - path: <required>
        role: <required — primary|manifests|secrets|...>
```

Shorthand for single-workspace:
```yaml
projects:
  - name: ibkr-mcp
    path: /home/coding/ibkr-mcp  # role defaults to 'primary'
```

## Acceptance
- Schema validates both shapes
- Migration from v0.1 single-workspace to v0.2 multi-workspace is lossless
- Unit tests cover edge cases (empty workspaces array rejected; duplicate roles allowed but warned)

## Plan reference
§4.2 Project registry (multi-workspace model)

title: Ad-hoc vs fleet classification: data model + UI filter
description:
## Task
Every conversation classified as `fleet` (has `[needle:...]` prefix) or `operator` / `ad-hoc` (no prefix). Stored on the Stitch record. Filter controls in UI.

## Acceptance
- Classification at Stitch-creation time (worker Stitch from tagged session = fleet; any others = operator-kind)
- UI filter persists in per-operator UI state
- Analytics panels separate fleet cost from ad-hoc cost

## Plan reference
§5.3 Ad-hoc vs fleet, §6 Phase 2 deliverable 6

title: Unassigned-conversation bucket (sessions outside registered projects)
description:
## Task
Sessions discovered in CLI directories that don't match any registered project get surfaced in an "Unassigned" bucket, not silently dropped.

## Acceptance
- UI shows Unassigned bucket with session list
- Per-session actions: assign to project, or ignore permanently
- Memory cost bounded (cap entries; older evicted to disk)

## Plan reference
§5.4 Session tailer filtering

title: Search palette: cross-project with project badges
description:
## Task
Upgrade Phase 1 search palette to search across all registered projects. Each result badged with project.

## Acceptance
- Results limited to 50 total, balanced across projects
- Project filter tokens (`project:kalshi-weather`) narrow results
- Case-insensitive substring match; 150ms debounce

## Plan reference
§4.5 Search palette, §6 Phase 2 deliverable 8

title: Cost aggregator: per-project/adapter/model/strand/day buckets
description:
## Task
Parse usage fields from each adapter's session files. Aggregate into rolling buckets: per-project, per-adapter, per-model, per-strand, per-day.

## Acceptance
- Claude: message.usage input/output/cache-read/cache-write
- Codex: token_count events
- OpenCode: per-message tokens + cost fields
- Gemini: native usage fields
- Pricing from config (not hardcoded — prior-art lesson)
- Totals within ±2% of `br list --json` summaries

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 2 deliverable 9 (Cost panel), notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §I

title: Rate-limit window overlay for Claude (5h + 7d) on cost chart
description:
## Task
On the cost chart, overlay the Claude Max 5-hour and 7-day rolling windows. Visual indicator of approach-to-limit.

## Acceptance
- Windows rendered as semi-transparent shaded regions
- Approach-to-limit (≥80%) highlighted
- Window computation matches §16 capacity metrics

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 2 deliverable 9

title: Cost-per-closed-Stitch unit economics view
description:
## Task
A derived metric: aggregated cost of all beads linked to a Stitch, divided by 1 (since a Stitch is the atomic work unit). Trended over time.

## Acceptance
- Per-Stitch cost surfaced in Stitch detail
- Trend line over last 30/90/180 days
- Grouped by adapter and by project for comparison

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 2 deliverable 9, §16.7 business metrics (cost_per_stitch_usd)

title: Per-account utilization: Claude 5h/7d meters from local JSONL
description:
## Task
Parse Claude Code's session JSONL usage fields. Compute per-account 5h and 7d rolling utilization. Match what `/status` shows within ±5%.

## Why
This is the equivalent of the "claude governor" / `/status` signal the operator wanted surfaced. HOOP displays it — never enforces. (Per §8 non-goal #3.)

## Acceptance
- Per-account meter (one account = one credential dir)

## Task

## Rules

- `Awaiting Review` — review-kind linked beads open
- `Quiet N days` — no activity for N days (N shown to user)

## Why
Stitches descend into obscurity (Reddit model); status is a hint, not a lifecycle terminus. (§1.6, §8.14 non-goal)

## Acceptance
- Pure function; property test: same inputs → same output
- Status derivation: 10ms max for a Stitch with 20 linked beads
- Backward-compat: derivation doesn't require phase 4+ data

close_reason:
Implemented pure function for Stitch status derivation in hoop-daemon/src/stitch_status.rs

Summary:
- Pure function StitchContext::derive_status() computes status from linked beads + activity
- Status rules: In Progress (claimed bead OR recent streaming), Awaiting Review (open review beads), Quiet N days (no activity)
- Comprehensive tests: property test (same inputs → same output), performance test (< 10ms for 20 beads), backward compatibility tests
- Never stored; computed on read per §1.6, §4.7

Note: Codebase has unrelated compilation errors in other files (supervisor.rs, capacity.rs, events.rs) that prevent cargo test from running, but stitch_status.rs itself is complete and correct.

title: Phase 3: File browser + artifact preview + multimodal (v0.3)
description:
# Phase 3: File browser + artifact preview + multimodal (v0.3)

**Version target:** v0.3 | **Timeline:** +16 weeks

## Goal
Operator can browse any project's files, preview code / docs / images / media with syntax highlighting, and supply multimodal input (text / image / audio / video) for Stitch drafting and agent conversations. Two capture surfaces: Dictated Notes (pure note-taking) and Voice/Screen Work Capture (drafts a Stitch).

## Why this phase exists
Humans think with their hands in the code and their voice while walking. A text-only UI misses both. The dictation surface specifically — audio + transcript as first-class entities — captures *why* decisions were made at the moment they were made, which is the most valuable record of real work.

## Scope
- Per-project file browser (tree, filter, mtime/size/git-status)
- Syntax highlighting (server `syntect` + client Shiki)
- Non-text preview: images, PDFs, audio, video, binary hex dump
- Artifact-aware links (bead view → files touched → file browser at right revision)
- Multimodal input to Stitch drafts + agent chat
- Streaming upload with progress + resumability
- Path-sensitive routing (drag file into draft → picks up path + revision + snippet)
- **Marquee #5:** Dictated Notes (first-class conversation entity; audio + Whisper transcript in project timeline)
- **Marquee #6:** Voice / Screen Work Capture (builds on Notes; synthesizes Stitch draft)

## Non-goals
- File editing via UI (read-only)
- Rich cloud-sync of attachments (local only; S3 backup in phase 6 covers archival)

## Key constraints
- Mobile UX matters here — phone-ADB dictation is a primary use case
- Streaming content in a separate reactive atom (in-flight isolation rule)
- Attachment size limits and storage layout matter: audio grows fast

## References
- Plan §6 Phase 3
- Plan §21 (Mobile UX)
- Plan §18 (Privacy & redaction applies heavily here)

## Closing criteria
- File browser <1s directory-expand on 20k-file repo
- Syntax highlighting for Rust/TS/Python/Go/Clojure/YAML/TOML/Markdown/Shell/SQL/Dockerfile
- Image/audio/video preview in Safari/Chrome/Firefox
- 10MB image attachment stored and referenced in a Stitch draft
- End-to-end voice capture on Pixel 6 → transcribed Note in project timeline <60s
