title: Phase 4: Stitch creation interface (v0.4)
description:
# Phase 4: Stitch creation interface — HOOP's single write path (v0.4)

**Version target:** v0.4 | **Timeline:** +22 weeks

## Goal
HOOP gains its single write path: creating Stitches (backed by `br create`) via UI form, chat draft, and templates / bulk. Every `br create` is audited, previewed, and decomposes operator intent into the right beads with the right labels. Three marquee features land: "What Will This Take?" preview, Already-Started Detection, Stitch Replay from Failure Point.

## Why this phase exists
The operator's write surface. Until now HOOP is an observer; phase 4 makes it useful as a drafter. The "one write path" invariant means phase 4 is simultaneously the riskiest (it's the only place HOOP touches the queue) and the most constrained (everything happens via `br create` with labels).

## Scope
- Form-based Stitch draft (target project, title, description, kind, deps, labels, attachments, preview)
- Template library (`~/.hoop/templates/` and `<project>/.hoop/templates/`)
- Submit flow: draft → preview → `br create --json` with `stitch:<id>` label → audit row → event → UI redirect
- Chat-driven drafting (lightweight, pre-agent; Haiku-class or rule-based)
- Bulk draft (paste markdown list, split into previewable drafts; hard cap 50)
- Audit trail with `actor` + `source` fields
- **Marquee #7:** "What Will This Take?" Preview (cost p50/p90, duration, risk, conflicts — BEFORE submit)
- **Marquee #8:** Already-Started Detection (semantic pre-dedup across open Stitches)
- **Marquee #9:** Stitch Replay from Failure Point (reconstruct state; resume as new bead or continue in agent)

## Explicit non-actions
- HOOP does NOT `close`, `update`, `claim`, `release`, or any other `br` verb beyond `create`
- Strand is never a routing factor (strands are worker-immutable; the dry-run matches by adapter/model/historical-fit only)

## Critical design anchors
- Every bead HOOP creates carries `stitch:<stitch-id>` label
- Every `br create` produces an audit row in `fleet.db`
- Preview flow is gated — no silent submits
- Bulk ops have a hard ceiling with explicit override

## References
- Plan §6 Phase 4
- Plan §2.1 (`br` dependency — HOOP shells out, never touches `.beads/` directly)
- Plan §4.7 (Stitch service schema)

## Closing criteria
- Form-drafted Stitch appears in NEEDLE's queue and a worker claims it
- Audit row exists for every HOOP-created bead
- Chat-driven drafting produces reasonable first drafts
- Bulk splits 10-item markdown into 10 previewable drafts
- Dry-run estimates within p50/p90 bands for 80% of closed Stitches after 30 days
- Already-Started catches synthetic cross-project duplicate with >95% recall
- Replay reconstructs failure moment and resumes successfully

title: Stitch draft form: project + title + description + kind + deps + attachments
description:
## Task
The primary UI for creating a Stitch. Form fields: target project (required), title (required), description (markdown), kind (default task; genesis/review/fix selectable), dependencies (pick from existing; searchable), priority (default inferred from queue), assignee hint (optional), labels, attachments (from phase 3).

## Acceptance
- All required fields enforced at UI layer
- Markdown body has live preview
- Dep picker supports type-ahead across project's open Stitches
- Cannot target a project with no workspaces
- Computes and shows the bead graph delta visually (what beads will be created)

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 4 deliverable 1

title: Stitch Replay: reconstruct failure state + resume-as-new-bead OR continue-in-agent
description:
## Task
When a Stitch's linked bead fails, offer two resume options:
1. **Resume as new bead** — HOOP creates a new bead with same `stitch:<id>` label, pre-populated with "pick up from step N" prompt + reconstructed context.
2. **Continue in agent** — (phase 5) the agent inherits the reconstructed state in its chat.

Reconstruction sources: NEEDLE events, CLI session JSONL, worktree git state at time of failure (via git stash-create hook from NEEDLE).

## Acceptance
- Reconstruction completes in <10s for a 30-minute-work-session failure
- Resume-as-new-bead tested: new bead picks up correctly; old failed bead stays closed as history
- Continue-in-agent: agent sees reconstructed state via MCP context (phase 5)
- NEEDLE hook: `git stash create` on bead failure, stash SHA recorded in events

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 4 marquee #9

title: NEEDLE hook #5: git stash-create on bead failure (for Stitch Replay)
description:
## Task
Coordinated with NEEDLE: on bead failure, NEEDLE runs `git stash create` in the worker's worktree and records the stash SHA in the failure event. HOOP uses this for Stitch Replay reconstruction.

## Acceptance
- NEEDLE PR merged
- stash-create is fast (<2s) and non-destructive
- Stash SHAs accessible via `git stash show` for Replay reconstruction
- Graceful degradation if stash fails (Replay still offers reconstruction from events + transcripts, just without worktree state)

## Plan reference
Notes/interop-with-needle.md, §6 Phase 4 marquee #9

title: Zero other br verbs invariant: compile-time + runtime enforcement for phase 4+
description:
## Task
After phase 4 adds `br create`, ensure ONLY `br create` is called. Compile-time feature gating + runtime subprocess-arg inspection as belt-and-suspenders.

## Acceptance
- Any code path attempting `br close/update/release/...` fails to compile
- Runtime guard rejects non-`create` br invocations
- CI test: fake br stub that logs all verbs; assert only `create` is ever called during normal operation

## Plan reference
§3.8 Principle (br create is HOOP's only write), §8.5 Non-goal, §6 Phase 4 non-action list

title: CLI: hoop new <project> — shortcut for draft + submit via terminal
description:
## Task
CLI subcommand that opens a draft in \$EDITOR (markdown with YAML frontmatter). On save, validates and submits via daemon control socket.

## Acceptance
- Opens default editor with template
- Validates YAML frontmatter
- Dry-run available (`--dry-run`)
- Audit row includes `source: cli`

## Plan reference
§4.1 Subcommands (hoop new)

title: Stitch submit flow: draft → preview → br create → audit → redirect
description:
## Task
On submit, daemon:
1. Validates draft against schema
2. Decomposes into bead payload(s) via Stitch decomposition service
3. Shells `br create --json <payload>` with `stitch:<stitch-id>` label, for each bead, in project cwd
4. Inserts audit row with actor + source
5. Emits `stitch_created` event on WS
6. UI redirects to the new Stitch view

## Acceptance
- Submit is atomic per-bead (partial failures roll back audit; no orphaned Stitch rows)
- Actor identity from Tailscale whois (§13)
- Source field records `form` | `chat` | `bulk` | `template:<name>`
- Full round-trip tested in `testrepo/`

## Plan reference
§5.2 Bead creation data flow, §6 Phase 4 deliverable 3

title: Stitch template library (~/.hoop/templates + <project>/.hoop/templates)
description:
## Task
Reusable Stitch templates. Markdown files with frontmatter describing the template (title pattern, default description, default beads). Two scopes: global and project-scoped.

## Acceptance
- Templates discovered on startup + file-watched for hot-reload
- Template picker in draft form
- Per-template required fields surface as form inputs
- Example templates shipped: "review-PR", "fix-from-incident", "investigate-failure"

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 4 deliverable 2, §22 Extensibility (prompts-like pattern)

title: Chat-driven drafting (pre-agent, lightweight): NL intent → Stitch draft
description:
## Task
Lightweight chat pane per project. Operator types natural language intent; system proposes a Stitch draft. Always routes through preview flow — never auto-submits.

Phase 4 uses either a small model (Haiku) or rule-based NL parsing. Phase 5 replaces with the full human-interface agent.

## Acceptance
- Chat pane per project
- Natural intent → reasonable first draft for common patterns
- Draft always editable before submit
- Never submits silently

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 4 deliverable 4

title: Bulk draft: paste markdown list → N previewable drafts (hard cap 50)
description:
## Task
Paste a bullet list or markdown doc; split into multiple drafts for individual review + submit.

## Acceptance
- Hard cap: 50 drafts per bulk op (explicit override flag)
- Each draft previewable + editable independently
- Submit all / submit selected / cancel
- Partial failure handling: committed + failed tracked distinctly

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 4 deliverable 5

title: Audit trail: every HOOP-created bead has actor + source + timestamp in fleet.db
description:
## Task
Every `br create` HOOP performs writes an actions audit row: ts, actor (operator email/identity), source (form/chat/bulk/template), project, Stitch id, bead id, args hash.

## Acceptance
- 1:1 correspondence between audits and HOOP-created beads
- Hash chain enforced (§13)
- Actor from Tailscale whois (phase 7 multi-operator adds per-operator identity)
- Queryable via REST `/api/audit`

## Plan reference
§5.2 Data flow, §13 Security (hash chain)

title: Stitch decomposition service: intent → bead graph (primary + review + fix)
description:
## Task
Translate a Stitch draft into the right bead graph. For an "investigation" Stitch → [one task bead, one review bead depending on it]. For a "fix" Stitch with acceptance criteria → tests bead + fix bead + review bead.

## Acceptance
- Kind-specific decomposition rules (configurable in config.yml)
- Rules are data-driven (not hard-coded in Rust)
- Operator can override bead graph at preview time
- Decomposed beads inherit the Stitch's workspace scope

## Plan reference
§4.7 Stitch service decomposition, §6 Phase 4

title: "What Will This Take?" dry-run preview service
description:
## Task
Before submit, HOOP simulates the Stitch and shows: likely-claimer (adapter + model, by historical fit — **not** strand), cost p50/p90 from similar Stitches, duration p50/p90, risk-pattern match, file-overlap conflicts.

## Acceptance
- Similarity function: lexical on title/body/labels; embedding in phase 5
- Cost/duration percentile from last 90d
- Risk pattern from Fix Lineage library (phase 2 marquee #4)
- Strand NEVER appears as a routing factor (§8.4 non-goal)
- UI preview card renders within 2s of draft change

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 4 marquee #7, §8.4 Non-goal (no strand routing)

title: Already-Started Detection: semantic pre-dedup at draft time
description:
## Task
On draft, embed the title+description. Search against open Stitches across all projects. If cosine similarity > threshold, interrupt draft flow with: "this looks like `<project>/<stitch>`, which is in progress. Continue that, add this as a child, or proceed as new?"

## Acceptance
- Local embedding model (gte-small or similar CPU-bound); no external API
- In-memory vector index rebuilt on bead/Stitch events
- Threshold configurable (default 0.82 cosine sim)
- False positive rate <5% tracked over first 30d
- Test: synthetic cross-project duplicate caught with >95% recall

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 4 marquee #8

title: Phase 5: The human-interface agent (v0.5)
description:
# Phase 5: Human-interface agent — the conversational handle (v0.5)

**Version target:** v0.5 | **Timeline:** +28 weeks

## Goal
Seat the human-interface agent: a persistent cross-project conversational partner that reviews artifacts, answers questions, and drafts Stitches. The operator's primary interface to HOOP once this lands. Plus three marquee features: Morning Brief, Cross-Project Stitch Propagation, Reflection Ledger.

## Why this phase exists
Until phase 5, HOOP is a good dashboard. Phase 5 makes it a coordinator. The agent is the difference between "see fleets" and "talk to a coordinator who helps run fleets." Without it, HOOP is valuable but not transformative.

## Scope
- Persistent Claude Code (or any adapter) agent session
- MCP server exposing HOOP's read APIs + one write (`create_stitch`)
- Agent tool belt — one write, many reads (see non-actions below)
- Notification channel (agent receives events, decides what to surface)
- Operator ↔ agent chat pane (primary UI surface of v0.5)
- Agent-off switch (HOOP works without agent enabled)
- Audit trail: `actor: hoop:agent:<session>` on every agent-drafted Stitch
- **LLM-agnostic:** Claude Code / Anthropic API / ZAI+GLM / any NEEDLE-compatible adapter via `config.yml`
- **Marquee #10:** Morning Brief (autonomous; structured briefing + pre-drafted Stitches + one headline)
- **Marquee #11:** Cross-Project Stitch Propagation (sibling-project detection)
- **Marquee #12:** Reflection Ledger (HOOP learns from repetition; proposes rules)

## Agent tool belt (canonical)
- `create_stitch(project, title, description, kind, attachments)` — the one write
- `find_stitches`, `read_stitch`, `find_beads` (expert), `read_bead` (expert)
- `read_file(project, path, revision)`, `grep(project, pattern)`
- `search_conversations(query, project?)`
- `summarize_project(project)`, `summarize_day()`
- `escalate_to_operator(message)` — UI banner; no auto-actions
- **Forbidden:** `launch_fleet`, `stop_fleet`, `release_claim`, `boost_priority`, `close_stitch`, `close_bead`

## Critical design anchors
- Lazy context (§3.12) — thin index by default, details via tool calls
- Stitch drafts always through preview flow (§3.10 read-first default)
- Hard ceilings (max_attempts, max_review_rounds, cycle_budget) enforced at draft time
- Cross-project chat requires explicit project targeting or UI confirmation
- If Mayor is said anywhere in code, UI, or docs: remove — the role is "human-interface agent"

## References
- Plan §6 Phase 5
- Plan §1.6 (Stitches and Patterns)
- Plan §4.7 (Reflection Ledger schema)
- Plan §13 (Security — MCP socket binding)

## Closing criteria
- Agent session survives `systemctl restart hoop` with context intact
- Cross-project summary correct in Stitch language
- Agent never performs a worker action
- Morning Brief produces useful daily summary with a correctly-scoped pre-drafted Stitch
- Cross-project propagation hits a real sibling across 3+ projects
- Reflection proposals flag repeated instructions and are operator-approvable

title: Agent adapter abstraction: Claude Code / Anthropic API / ZAI+GLM / plug-compatible
description:
## Task
Define the agent adapter interface. Implementations: Claude Code (default), Anthropic API, ZAI proxy with GLM models. Interface shape matches NEEDLE worker adapters so operators learn one schema.

## Adapter interface
- `spawn_session(config) → SessionId`
- `resume_session(SessionId) → Session`
- `send_turn(session, prompt, attachments) → stream<Event>`
- `close_session(session)`
- Events match the unified runtime union (session.started, text.delta, tool.use, etc.)

## Acceptance
- All three adapters implemented
- Config-driven selection from config.yml `agent.adapter`
- Adapter switch requires no code change
- Event shape identical across adapters (test fixture)

## Plan reference
§7 Tech decisions (LLM-agnostic), §5 Phase 5, §1.6 Roles

title: Cross-Project Stitch Propagation: fix-pattern detection across sibling projects
description:
## Task
Agent recognizes when a fix applied in one project has structural siblings in others (same config shape, same file layout, recent failure signals). Drafts matching Stitches for each sibling project (preview queue).

## Acceptance
- Structural similarity detected via file-pattern match (not embedding — too noisy for this)
- Sibling list surfaced to agent alongside closed-fix Stitch
- Proposed drafts per project always through preview
- False-positive rate tracked manually over first 30d

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 5 marquee #11

title: Reflection Ledger schema: reflection_ledger table + scope model
description:
## Task
fleet.db table:
```
reflection_ledger(id, scope, rule, reason, source_stitches, status,
                  created_at, last_applied, applied_count)
  scope ∈ {global, project:<name>, pattern:<id>}
  status ∈ {proposed, approved, rejected, archived}
```

## Acceptance
- Migration lands as 1.4.0 (minor)
- Full-text index on rule for search
- Source-stitches is a JSON array of IDs
- Applied_count incremented atomically on each injection

## Plan reference
§4.7 Reflection service, §20 Schema evolution

title: Reflection detector: scan closed operator Stitches for repeated patterns
description:
## Task
Async background job. After each closed operator Stitch, scan recent operator Stitches (window configurable, default 30d) for:
- Repeated corrections (same correction, 3+ instances)
- Repeated preferences (same preference stated 3+ times)
- Repeated negatives ("don't do X", 3+ times)
- Repeated approvals of non-obvious choices (same acceptance signal, 3+ times)

Detected patterns become `status=proposed` rows in the Reflection Ledger.

## Acceptance
- Runs within 2 minutes of closure
- Uses small local model for similarity grouping (or lexical if local model unavailable)
- Proposal rate expected: 1-3 per day under normal operator use
- Tested against synthetic repeated-instruction fixtures

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 5 marquee #12

title: Reflection Ledger UI: proposal pane with approve/edit/reject
description:
## Task
Dedicated UI pane listing pending proposals. Each shows: suggested rule, reason, source Stitches (clickable). Actions: approve (optionally edit first), reject (optional reason), defer.

## Acceptance
- Proposals surfaced within minute of detection
- Editing a proposal before approval applies the edit
- Approved rules show up in the applied-rules pane
- Rejected proposals re-appear only after another N signals

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 5 marquee #12

title: Reflection Ledger injection: approved rules → every new agent session's lazy-context index
description:
## Task
On new agent session start, approved rules (scoped to relevant project/Pattern + global) are injected into the lazy-context index. Each rule one line; scope noted. Agent sees them as a "standing instructions" list.

## Acceptance
- Injection respects scope (project-scoped rules only for that project's context)
- `applied_count` + `last_applied` updated atomically
- Session log records which rules were injected
- Archived rules do NOT inject

## Plan reference
§3.13 Principle (learn from repetition), §6 Phase 5 marquee #12

title: Agent audit trail: actor hoop:agent:<session> on every drafted Stitch
description:
## Task
Every Stitch the agent drafts carries `actor: hoop:agent:<session-id>` in the audit log, with a back-reference to the turn that produced it.

## Acceptance
- Audit row includes the agent's model + adapter
- Turn reference clickable in audit UI
- Reconstructing any drafted Stitch back to its origin chat turn works

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 5 deliverable 8

title: Agent session lifecycle: spawn / persist / attach-on-restart / resume-on-adapter-switch
description:
## Task
Persistent agent session hosted by HOOP. Session ID tracked in fleet.db. On daemon restart, HOOP reattaches to the existing session (via adapter's native session store). Adapter switch (e.g. Anthropic outage → ZAI failover) starts a new session but carries forward the Reflection Ledger + recent-activity context.

## Acceptance
- Session survives `systemctl --user restart hoop`
- Adapter switch mid-stream: new session, old transcript archived as a Stitch
- Session age + cost tracked
- `agent-off switch` cleanly disables without leaving orphaned session

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 5 deliverable 1, 7

title: MCP server exposing HOOP's read APIs + create_stitch tool
description:
## Task
Stand up `hoop-mcp` as a separate binary (per plan §9.3 open question resolution). Exposes:

- Read tools: find_stitches, read_stitch, find_beads, read_bead, read_file, grep, search_conversations, summarize_project, summarize_day
- One write tool: create_stitch (internally issues `br create` calls)
- Utility: escalate_to_operator

MCP socket on Unix domain socket with same user:group (§13 security).

## Acceptance
- All tools documented with input/output JSON schemas
- Adapter-independent (any MCP-capable CLI can connect)
- No forbidden verbs expose (§6 Phase 5 canonical tool belt)
- Audit log records every tool call with args hash

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 5 deliverable 3, §9.3 Open question (MCP in/out of process)

title: Agent context — lazy-fetch via tools, NOT eager-injection
description:
## Task
Agent's system prompt contains only a thin index: project names, recent activity summary (last N closed Stitches with titles), open Stitch titles, active alerts. File contents, full bead bodies, conversation transcripts are fetched via tool calls on demand.

## Why
Principle §3.12. Eager context overflows windows on long sessions + costs money for data that might never be relevant.

## Acceptance
- System prompt <4KB token budget
- All detail retrievable via tools
- Context-budget watchdog emits warning at 75% window usage
- Test: agent successfully answers "what happened today?" using only tool calls

## Plan reference
§3.12 Principle, §6 Phase 5 deliverable 2

title: Agent chat pane: primary UI surface for v0.5; cross-project by design
description:
## Task
Operator ↔ agent chat UI. Multimodal input (from phase 3). Streams in real time. Cross-project scope; per-project pinning optional.

## Acceptance
- Real-time token stream (in-flight isolation — separate reactive atom)
- Attachment picker works (phase 3 integration)
- Project context pill shows which project(s) are in scope
- Mobile-friendly (§21 Mobile UX)

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 5 deliverable 5, §21

title: Agent-drafted Stitch flow routes through phase 4 preview (no direct submits)
description:
## Task
When the agent calls `create_stitch`, the call doesn't immediately `br create`. It creates a draft that surfaces in the operator's preview queue. Operator approves → submit. Operator edits → new draft version. Operator rejects → draft discarded.

## Acceptance
- Agent never silently creates beads
- Draft queue persists across restarts
- Preview action audited (who approved, when)
- Rejection reason optional but captured

## Plan reference
§3.10 Principle (read-first), §6 Phase 5 deliverable 6, §6 Phase 4 preview flow

title: Agent notification channel: fleet events → structured notifications to agent
description:
## Task
When a Stitch's linked beads close, a convoy completes, or a capacity alert fires, emit a structured event to the agent's context. Agent decides whether to surface to operator via `escalate_to_operator` or silently note.

## Acceptance
- Events delivered within 5s of trigger
- Event schema stable and documented
- Agent has history of recent events (last 20) without consuming tool calls
- Test: synthetic event delivered and agent responds correctly

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 5 deliverable 4

title: Forbidden agent tools assertion: no launch/stop/release/close/boost verbs
description:
## Task
MCP server's tool registration explicitly excludes worker-steering verbs. Runtime check: if the agent ever attempts to invoke a forbidden tool, return a clear error explaining the non-goal.

## Acceptance
- Forbidden list hard-coded: launch_fleet, stop_fleet, release_claim, boost_priority, close_stitch, close_bead, signal_worker, kill_worker, pause_worker
- Requests to these return: "HOOP cannot perform worker-steering actions. To close a bead, use `br close` directly. To stop a worker, use NEEDLE's tooling."
- Tests verify each forbidden verb rejects correctly

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 5 deliverable 3 (canonical forbidden list), §8.2 Non-goal

title: Morning Brief: autonomous daily briefing + pre-drafted Stitches + one headline
description:
## Task
Scheduled agent run (default: on login or configurable cron). Agent reviews overnight activity across projects, produces:
1. Structured briefing: closes, failures, stuck items, anomalies, blocked-on-human
2. Pre-drafted Stitches (through preview flow — no auto-submits) for follow-ups
3. One headline decision with evidence

## Acceptance
- Brief generated in <3 minutes of wall time
- Output markdown-structured and easy to skim on mobile
- Drafted Stitches populate preview queue
- Delivered via WS notification + optional push (phase 7)

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 5 marquee #10

title: Phase 6: Operational polish (v0.6)
description:
# Phase 6: Operational polish (v0.6)

**Version target:** v0.6 | **Timeline:** +32 weeks

## Goal
Make HOOP pleasant to run for the long haul. systemd, hot-reload, backups, metrics, Tailscale identity, performance budgets.

## Why this phase exists
A tool the operator doesn't want to babysit is a tool they actually use. Phase 6 is where HOOP graduates from "interesting prototype" to "thing I trust to run unattended."

## Scope
- systemd user service template
- Config hot-reload (§17)
- Log rotation
- `/healthz` + `/readyz`
- Daily `fleet.db` snapshot
- Drop-in binary upgrade flow
- Prometheus `/metrics` (§16)
- Tailscale-aware auth (inherited from environment; §13)
- Performance budget: 20 projects × 5 workers × 300 beads, UI responsive
- Graceful degradation on per-project failures

## Closing criteria
- `systemctl --user restart hoop` resumes state in <5s
- Bad `config.yml` edit rejected; old config keeps running
- One month of normal operation produces <1GB in logs+backups
- Operator identity visible in audit log for every mutation

title: systemd user service template + install command
description:
## Task
`hoop install-systemd` writes `~/.config/systemd/user/hoop.service`. Service restarts on failure with rate limiting. Journal integration via `journalctl --user -u hoop`.

## Acceptance
- Unit file correct (Type=simple, Restart=on-failure, TimeoutStartSec, TimeoutStopSec)
- Rate limit: max 5 restarts per 5 min
- Documented in README + docs/operations.md

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 6 deliverable 1

title: Tailscale identity integration (whois lookup for operator ID)
description:
## Task
HTTP/WS connections from Tailscale clients have their peer identity resolved via `tailscale whois`. Identity stored in session + audit rows.

## Acceptance
- `whois` invocation cached per connection (not re-run per request)
- Falls back to OS user when Tailscale identity unavailable
- Audit log shows email (e.g. `jed@...`) instead of IP

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 6 deliverable 8, §13 Security (Tailscale identity)

title: Performance budget verification in CI (20 projects × 5 workers × 300 beads)
description:
## Task
CI load test harness asserts: UI responsive (<500ms interaction) and memory <4GB under the target load. Budget violations block merge.

## Acceptance
- testrepo/ populated with the load case
- Playwright measures interaction latencies
- Memory ceiling measured via rss snapshots
- CI green = within budget; red = exceeded

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 6 deliverable 9

title: Observer-mode second instance (read-only attach to primary)
description:
## Task
A second `hoop serve --observer` on the same host opens its own UI but attaches to the primary's event streams read-only. Useful for long-tail debugging without disrupting primary.

## Acceptance
- Observer instance has no write path
- Shares primary's projections but doesn't mutate state
- Distinct UI port
- Clean detach on observer stop

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 6 deliverable 11

title: /healthz and /readyz endpoints for external monitors
description:
## Task
`/healthz` returns 200 if the process is responsive. `/readyz` returns 200 only when all per-project runtimes have started (or been explicitly marked degraded).

## Acceptance
- Response time <50ms
- No authentication required (intended for local or Tailscale-internal monitors)
- `/readyz` can return 503 with JSON body naming degraded projects

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 6 deliverable 4

title: Prometheus /metrics endpoint with full catalog (§16)
description:
## Task
Implement the metrics catalog from plan §16: operational, event ingestion, WS/HTTP, bead/Stitch operations, agent/AI, storage, business metrics. Prometheus text format.

## Acceptance
- All catalogued metrics emit correctly
- Cardinality bounded (no runaway labels)
- Scrape tested with Prometheus
- Grafana dashboard JSON included in `ops/dashboards/`

## Plan reference
§16 Self-observability

title: /debug/state JSON endpoint for incident triage
description:
## Task
Endpoint returns full runtime state as JSON: fleet roster, open Stitches, agent session IDs, observed PIDs, WS clients, config hash, backup timestamps, version info.

## Acceptance
- Local-only access (binds to UDS or 127.0.0.1)
- Response reliable under load
- Documented in troubleshooting guide

## Plan reference
§16.8 /debug/state

title: Log rotation: HOOP logs rotate at 100MB or daily; keep 14 days
description:
## Task
HOOP's own logs rotate via size or time trigger. Journal integration is preferred; fallback to file-based rotation with lumberjack-style library.

## Acceptance
- Default path: `~/.hoop/logs/` for file-based
- Rotation on 100MB or 24h (whichever first)
- Kept for 14 days then deleted
- Log line redaction applied at write time (§18)

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 6 deliverable 3

title: Backup service: daily fleet.db snapshot + incremental attachments sync to S3
description:
## Task
Scheduled backup per `config.yml backup.schedule`. Operations:
1. `VACUUM INTO` on fleet.db to temp snapshot
2. zstd-compress
3. Optional age-encrypt (if key in env)
4. Upload to configured S3 endpoint + prefix
5. Incremental sync of attachments (only new/changed since last success)
6. Write manifest.json tying snapshot together + schema version

## Acceptance
- Schedule honored (cron-format)
- Retry on transient failure (exponential backoff, max 3)
- Last-success timestamp recorded + exposed via metric
- Encryption reliable (age key via env only)

## Plan reference
§15 Backups & disaster recovery

title: hoop restore --from s3://<bucket>/<snapshot-id> command
description:
## Task
Restore from a prior snapshot. Requires daemon stopped. Moves existing `~/.hoop/` aside for rollback. Fetches snapshot, verifies manifest, restores fleet.db + attachments + config. Runs schema migrations to bring up to current version.

## Acceptance
- Precondition check: refuses if daemon running
- Snapshot manifest validated before destructive action
- Rollback available if restore fails mid-flight
- Schema version mismatch handled (refuses newer-than-current snapshots per §20)

## Plan reference
§15.4 Restore flow

title: Drop-in binary upgrade flow with state-preserving restart
description:
## Task
Operator curl-installs a new binary and `systemctl --user restart hoop`. Schema migrations run automatically. State preserved.

## Acceptance
- Restart resumes in <5s
- Schema migrations logged with durations
- Breaking-change upgrades refuse without explicit `hoop migrate --major-upgrade` command (§20)
- `hoop --version` exposes current version cleanly

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 6 deliverable 6, §20 Schema evolution

title: Hot-reload validator for config.yml and projects.yaml
description:
## Task
On file change, schema-validate the new content. On valid: apply. On invalid: reject loudly (UI banner + metric + log) and keep previous config.

## Acceptance
- Bad YAML rejected; old config continues running
- Bad schema rejected with specific error (field X: expected string, got number)
- UI banner persists until the operator fixes the file
- Test: every field has a validation error scenario

## Plan reference
§17 Configuration hot-reload, §6 Phase 6 deliverable 2

title: Phase 7: Multi-operator (v1.0)
description:
# Phase 7: Multi-operator (v1.0)

**Version target:** v1.0 | **Timeline:** +36 weeks

## Goal
More than one person can use the same HOOP instance cleanly. Roles, identity, per-operator state, optional presence.

## Why this phase exists
v1.0 is the public milestone. Multi-operator is the last substantive capability before the tool is something a small team can adopt.

## Scope
- Roles: viewer (read-only) + drafter (read + create Stitches). Two levels only.
- Tailscale identity-based role assignment (§13, inherited auth)
- Audit log carries real operator identity on every bead creation
- Per-operator UI state (pinned projects, last-opened Stitch, filters)
- Optional presence indicators (privacy-toggleable)
- Stitch draft concurrency (§19): drafts server-persisted from open
- Reflection Ledger concurrency: proposal deduplication
- Public README + examples + user docs

## Closing criteria
- Two operators see consistent state at all times
- Viewer role cannot access bead-creation endpoint at schema boundary
- README enables a stranger to install HOOP against their own NEEDLE workspace in <30 min

title: Roles: viewer (read) + drafter (read + create); schema-boundary enforcement
description:
## Task
Two-role model. Viewer can't access the Stitch-creation endpoint at the schema boundary (returns 403 before any business logic). Drafter has full read + `br create` via HOOP.

## Acceptance
- Role enforcement at route-level middleware
- Viewer requests to write endpoints return 403 with clear message
- No "viewer can't do this" errors appear deep in the stack
- Role assigned per Tailscale identity in config

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 7 deliverable 1, §13 Security (inherited identity)

title: Server-persisted Stitch drafts (phase 7 concurrency requirement)
description:
## Task
From the moment the operator opens a draft form, the draft persists server-side (not just on save). Multiple operators opening drafts can't lose work to each other.

## Acceptance
- Draft row created on form open
- Autosave every 5s or on field change
- Submit converts draft to beads + audit rows
- Close-without-submit marks draft as abandoned (retained for 7d)

## Plan reference
§19.1 Draft concurrency

title: Reflection Ledger proposal dedup (content-hash on create)
description:
## Task
When two operators' sessions produce the same proposal concurrently, deduplicate on content hash. One row, not two.

## Acceptance
- Hash computed on `rule` text (normalized)
- Duplicate creation is a no-op
- Source_stitches array includes both operators' source Stitches

## Plan reference
§19.2 Reflection concurrency

title: Presence indicators (optional, privacy-toggleable)
description:
## Task
Per-project and per-Stitch presence dots showing which operators are currently viewing. Opt-out per operator.

## Acceptance
- Presence via WS heartbeat
- Opt-out hides operator from indicators but doesn't block them from using HOOP
- Clear privacy-toggle UI

## Plan reference
§19.4 Presence

title: Per-operator UI state persistence (pinned projects, last-opened, filters)
description:
## Task
Per-operator UI preferences in fleet.db. Pinned projects, last-opened Stitch, filter state per panel, theme preference.

## Acceptance
- Keyed by Tailscale identity (falls back to OS user)
- Persists across sessions
- Exported in backup manifest

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 7 deliverable 4

title: Public README polish + examples + v1.0 release prep
description:
## Task
Final pass on README.md. Add:
- Install example with specific version numbers
- Screenshots of real UI (anonymized)
- Demo video link (optional)
- Example fleet.yaml + config.yml + accounts.yaml for common patterns
- Contributing guidelines

## Acceptance
- README enables a stranger to install against their NEEDLE workspace in <30 min (phase 7 success criterion)
- Examples verified against testrepo/
- GitHub release notes drafted

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 7 deliverable 6

title: X: Onboarding & documentation (§12)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Onboarding & documentation

**Plan section:** §12

## Why this is cross-cutting
Onboarding isn't a phase — it lands alongside every new surface. A phase that ships without its onboarding piece ships broken for anyone but the author.

## Three surfaces
1. `hoop init` interactive CLI wizard (dep check → project reg → agent setup → systemd → health)
2. In-UI first-run experience (welcome overlay, guided tour, starter prompts)
3. Progressive capability introduction (tips when features go unused, what's-new on upgrade)

## Specific aids
- Explain-this hover (every non-obvious UI element has a one-liner)
- Dry-run for first Stitch drafts
- Optional sample-Stitches tour project
- Agent pre-priming with operator's actual data

## Repo documentation
- `README.md` at repo root (primary; present)
- `AGENTS.md` at repo root (LLM guide; present)
- `docs/plan/plan.md` (authoritative; present)
- `docs/concepts/` — one-pager per concept (future)
- `docs/operations.md` — systemd/backups/upgrades (future)
- `docs/troubleshooting.md` — mapped to `hoop audit` output (future)

## Principle
Onboarding is progressive — never front-loaded. An operator who only wants to observe their fleet shouldn't need to learn Patterns on day one.

## Closing criteria
All phases have their onboarding deliverables landed. Public README enables <30-min stranger setup.

title: hoop init interactive wizard: dep check + project reg + agent setup + systemd + health
description:
## Task
The first-time setup wizard. Walks through five stages:
1. Dependency check (runs `hoop audit`)
2. First project registration (offers `scan ~/` preview)
3. Agent adapter setup (optional; Anthropic/Claude Code/ZAI)
4. systemd install
5. Health check + URL print

## Acceptance
- Under 5 minutes to complete if prerequisites installed
- Fails loudly and specifically on missing deps
- Skippable steps marked as such
- Re-runnable (idempotent)

## Plan reference
§12 Onboarding, §6 Phase 1 onboarding row

title: In-UI first-run experience: welcome overlay + guided tour + starter prompts
description:
## Task
On first UI open, show welcome overlay explaining Stitches/Patterns briefly. Soft highlights on key panels. Three starter prompts: "dictate a first note", "register another project", "ask the agent something".

## Acceptance
- Dismissable; re-openable from settings
- One-time flag stored in per-operator UI state
- Each starter prompt is a single-click shortcut

## Plan reference
§12 Onboarding

title: Progressive capability introduction: unused-feature prompts + what's-new on upgrade
description:
## Task
- Upgrade introduces a feature → "What's new" card next login
- Reflection Ledger empty after 30d → "Want me to start proposing rules?"
- 10+ Stitches share a theme but no Pattern → suggest creating one
- Agent never used → inline prompt on chat pane
- Mic never used → prompt near hotkey icon

## Acceptance
- Each prompt fires exactly once unless re-triggered
- Dismissable
- Respects operator's "don't bug me" global setting

## Plan reference
§12 Onboarding (progressive introduction)

title: Explain-this hover: tooltip glossary on every non-obvious UI element
description:
## Task

## Acceptance

- Hover delay configurable (default 500ms)
- Mobile shows on long-press
- Tab-accessible via `?` keyboard shortcut

## Acceptance
